## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hey, this is Deepash and this video is going to show you one idea for setting up your post purchase targeting. So for this particular account, I'm actually targeting people that have not bought in the last 30 days and have not bought in 180. So if they've made a purchase, then I'm waiting 30 days to serve this particular set of ads. I'm giving them between 30 and 180 days to repurchase. So my repurchase audience is therefore bigger. This is one of the common mistakes that people make is making the audience size is too small and then suffering because your CPM is really increased and it's really hard to find the right people. So if I just quickly show you how I've set this one up, so the campaign level is a website conversion campaign. It's level four in my campaign setup for existing customers. And essentially what I've done is to create a custom audience and then to segment that audience. So essentially, if I just scroll down to my audience here, so I'm targeting people that have purchased in the past 180 days, and I'm excluding them if they've purchased in the last 30. So the segment is 30 to 180. And this this is done because if someone has purchased this particular product, which is a candle, I know through research, on average, it takes 30 to 60 days, maybe even 90 days to burn through the candle. And therefore they may be looking to buy another product. So this has to be specific to your your your type of product, your store, etc. But for this, we did some research. And we found out the right kind of point was 30 days after their last purchase. So this is for existing customers as well as new. And then what I wanted to kind of maximize the size of the audience, you can only currently go up to 180 days in terms of your website customer audience. And so that's what we've done here. You could go and create a custom audience from your own email database and extend it for all customers on your database, for example. But for this specific one, we've done 30 and 180. And that's it, we've left everyone in our targeting pool, regardless of gender and age. I've played around the few placements, generally, I turn all placements on. And that's pretty much it. So seven days after clicking or one day after viewing, that conversion window really depends on how many conversions you're getting a day, because this this particular setup, at the time we're doing seven days, and one day after viewing, as you start to get more conversions, you can rack up, but really depends on how much maturity your current Ecom setup has in your store has in terms of data. For this particular store, it had two to three years worth of selling and worth of customer data. So I base this budget therefore on testing. So I started off initially at $10 to $20. And

one of the key things I look at is the frequency, like how many, what kind of frequency am I getting on a seven day period? If I get a frequency of around two, I'm kind of okay with that. ## [09:07 - 10:54]

that gives you an idea of kind of what you can do with your post purchase sequence. This worked for this particular client because it was a pattern behavior. So I know generally within 30, 60, maybe 90 days, people will repurchase. And that's for you to test on your particular store to get that right. But I would start broad and, you know, for example, go 30 to 180 days, or maybe even 14 to 180 days, and start testing different offers. You could try, you know, one of the common ones is actually just a thank you message seven days after the purchase goes a long way. And I've actually tested this on another account, and it does work. Sometimes it just brings in sales. And there's no logical reason necessarily as to why that is. Just a thank you message could be a static image could be a fun image could be a founder or you know, just a person as a face of the business sharing a video just saying thanks for your purchase really great to have you as a customer and hope to see you again soon. Sometimes that just helps to just keep people front of mind. And it kind of gives a different personal experience as much as you can on Facebook. For that experience that says you know what this brand actually care, they actually thought of me as well. They're not actually pushing us out. In other instances, you can, you know, push below the surface market offers that you don't want to share to other other kind of cold prospects.
